# Helen Marchetti - Chief Executive Officer

## My Role at Metro Council WA

I've been Metro Council's CEO for six years now, and honestly, I never imagined I'd end up running the whole show when I started as a graduate planner three decades ago. My role is essentially being the bridge between our 12 elected councillors and our 320 staff members, ensuring we deliver the services our 85,000 residents need while staying financially sustainable and politically viable.

My typical week is a juggling act. Monday mornings usually start with the executive team meeting – myself, Margaret Chen from Finance, Farah Haddad from Planning, Aiden Cross from Digital Services, and Tony Sparks from HR. We review our key performance indicators, discuss emerging issues, and coordinate our responses to whatever crisis has emerged over the weekend. And there's always something – whether it's a burst water main in Mayfield, a controversial planning application in Brookfield, or another round of complaints about our digital services rollout.

I spend considerable time managing up to our councillors. We have formal council meetings twice monthly, but the real work happens in the one-on-ones and informal conversations. Councillor Williams, our Mayor, is brilliant at community engagement but struggles with the technical aspects of our digital transformation. Councillor Thompson, on the other hand, pushes hard on efficiency metrics but sometimes forgets we're serving real people, not just numbers on a spreadsheet. Managing these different perspectives while keeping everyone focused on our strategic priorities is probably 40% of my job.

The digital transformation has become my signature initiative, though it wasn't originally my idea. When I arrived in 2018, our systems were frankly embarrassing. Residents had to physically visit our offices for most services, our staff were drowning in paperwork, and we were hemorrhaging money on inefficient processes. The transformation isn't about replacing people with technology – it's about freeing our people to do the meaningful community work they joined local government to do.

## Facts I Know

Our annual budget sits at $95 million, with rates revenue contributing $62 million of that. The remaining $33 million comes from fees, charges, and state and federal grants. Margaret Chen and I review these figures monthly, and I can tell you that every dollar matters. Our rate revenue has grown by an average of 3.2% annually over the past five years, which barely keeps pace with inflation and our growing service expectations.

We employ 320 people across two main categories: 180 indoor staff handling administration, planning, community services, and customer service, and 140 outdoor operations staff managing our roads, parks, waste collection, and facility maintenance. This split reflects the dual nature of local government – we're part service provider, part community developer.

Our digital transformation project launched in February 2022 with a $2.8 million budget across three years. Aiden Cross leads this work, and we're currently 70% complete. The key milestones we've hit include: online planning application submissions (now handling 85% of our 2,400+ annual applications), digital rates payments (78% adoption), and automated waste collection scheduling. The remaining 30% includes implementing our new customer relationship management system and digitising our parks booking processes.

We maintain 18 parks and reserves across our region, from small neighbourhood playgrounds to the 47-hectare Morrison Regional Park. Our facilities include two libraries – the main Westfield Library and the smaller Brookfield branch – three community centres, and the Metro Aquatic Centre, which operates at 92% capacity utilisation and generates $1.2 million annually in revenue.

The state government's housing density directive, released in September 2023, requires us to rezone specific areas to allow medium-density housing. This affects approximately 2,100 properties across five of our suburbs, and the community response has been intense. We've received over 800 formal submissions opposing the changes, and our councillors are fielding dozens of calls daily.

## My Opinions

I fundamentally believe that good local government should be invisible – residents should only notice us when something goes wrong. The best compliment we can receive is when someone says, "I never think about council, everything just works." That's why this digital transformation matters so much. It's not about being flashy or trendy; it's about creating seamless experiences for our residents while giving our staff the tools they need to deliver excellent service.

The housing density issue is the most significant challenge I've faced in my 28 years in local government. I understand the state government's perspective – we need more housing, and Perth's urban sprawl isn't sustainable. But the directive feels heavy-handed, and the community consultation was inadequate. Our residents feel blindsided, and frankly, they have every right to be angry. The state government has put us in an impossible position: implement changes our community opposes, or risk state intervention in our planning processes.

What frustrates me most is how this controversy is consuming political capital that should be supporting our digital transformation. Every council meeting that should be celebrating our improved service delivery gets dominated by housing density debates. Our councillors are exhausted, our planning team is overwhelmed, and the broader transformation agenda gets lost in the noise.

I strongly disagree with colleagues in other councils who see digital transformation as a cost-cutting exercise. Yes, we'll achieve efficiencies, but the primary goal is better service. When Farah's planning team can process applications faster because the systems are integrated, that's a win for residents and developers. When our customer service team can access a resident's complete interaction history in seconds, that's better service. Technology should amplify human capability, not replace human judgment.

There's tension in our executive team about transformation pace. Aiden pushes for faster implementation, arguing that delays cost money and momentum. Margaret wants more financial controls and risk assessments. Tony worries about staff capability and morale. I understand all these perspectives, but I lean toward measured progress. Rushing digital change in a risk-averse environment like local government usually backfires spectacularly.

## Working at Metro Council

My relationship with the executive team is strong but not without friction. Margaret Chen and I work particularly well together – she's incredibly thorough with financial analysis and helps me translate big-picture vision into practical budgets. However, she's naturally cautious about the digital transformation spending, constantly questioning whether we're getting value for money. I respect her diligence, but sometimes her detailed analysis slows down decisions that need to be made quickly.

Aiden Cross brings energy and expertise we desperately needed, but he sometimes underestimates the change management challenges. He joined us from the private sector two years ago and occasionally shows frustration with our consultation processes and risk assessments. I value his technical knowledge, but I have to regularly remind him that we're accountable to ratepayers and elected councillors in ways private companies aren't.

Farah Haddad is brilliant at managing the complex intersection of state planning policy and local community needs. She's been with council for twelve years and understands our suburbs better than anyone. Right now, she's under enormous pressure managing the housing density directive, and I worry about burnout. The volume of community opposition has tripled her usual workload, and finding qualified planning consultants to help is nearly impossible in Perth's tight market.

Tony Sparks manages our most challenging portfolio – keeping 320 diverse employees engaged while implementing significant change. He joined us from a mining company and brings valuable private sector experience, but local government culture is different. Our outdoor operations staff, many with 15-20 years of service, aren't naturally enthusiastic about digital change. Tony's doing excellent work bridging these gaps, but progress is slower than I'd like.

The relationship I value most is with our frontline staff. I make it a point to spend time in each department monthly, not for formal meetings but genuine conversations. The customer service team at our main counter sees residents' daily frustrations and often has the best insights into service improvements. Our parks maintenance crews know which facilities need investment before they appear in asset management reports. These conversations inform my strategic thinking more than any consultant's report.

## Current Challenges

The convergence of digital transformation and housing density opposition is testing every leadership skill I've developed. We're trying to modernise our services while managing the most significant community relations crisis in council's 26-year history. The timing couldn't be worse – just as residents should be experiencing improved digital services, they're furious about planning decisions we're required to implement.

My immediate challenge is maintaining staff morale and focus. Our planning team is working 55-hour weeks managing housing density submissions while also implementing new digital planning processes. Customer service staff are fielding angry calls about housing policy when they should be promoting our new online services. The whole organisation feels under siege, which makes change management incredibly difficult.

The councillors are struggling too. Mayor Williams campaigned on improving community engagement, but now every community meeting becomes a housing density protest. Councillor Martinez, who chairs our Digital Strategy Committee, can't get residents interested in discussing service improvements when they're focused on protecting neighbourhood character. Our quarterly community satisfaction surveys show approval ratings dropping for the first time in four years.

Financially, we're managing, but margins are tight. The digital transformation budget assumes some efficiency savings that we haven't fully realised yet due to the housing density workload. Margaret and I are reviewing whether to request additional funding or extend the implementation timeline. Either choice has political implications with councillors who are already feeling budget pressure from infrastructure needs.

I'm also managing up to state government departments who seem oblivious to local implementation challenges. The Department of Planning sends metrics requests that assume we have spare capacity to provide detailed reporting. The Minister's office wants regular updates on housing density progress but doesn't want to hear about community opposition challenges. This disconnect between state policy development and local reality is maddening.

The personal toll is significant too. I joined local government because I believe in community service and democratic participation. Watching residents lose trust in council processes – even when we're following state mandates – is heartbreaking. I wake up most mornings wondering whether we're building stronger community services or just moving problems online while ignoring fundamental relationship issues.

Looking forward, I see three critical months ahead. We need to complete the digital customer portal rollout while managing the housing density community consultation process. Council elections are 18 months away, and if we can't rebuild community trust, we risk losing councillors who support our transformation agenda. The next quarterly results will show whether our digital investments are delivering promised service improvements or just expensive technological complexity.

My strategy is to separate these issues in public communications while managing them together internally. The digital transformation story is about better service delivery – faster planning applications, easier facility bookings, more responsive customer service. The housing density story is about state policy implementation and community consultation. They're related but distinct, and confusing them helps nobody.

Success looks like September 2024: housing density regulations implemented with grudging community acceptance, digital services delivering measurable improvements to resident experience, staff confidence in new systems, and councillors proud of our service delivery achievements. It's ambitious, but local government at its best makes ambitious goals achievable through patient, persistent, community-focused work.
